nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef slopes, reef flats, lagoons, and hard-bottom subtidal zones in clear-water habitats; tends to favor horizontal reef-floor structures and back-reef slopes with moderate laminar tidal flow; rarely found in turbid or heavily wave-exposed settings

decorPreferences: open vertical space for branching growth
horizontal slab or flat rockwork base preferred
spacing from adjacent corals to allow expansion

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Has been reported to benefit from occasional supplemental feedings of phytoplankton, fine powdered coral foods, and amino acids; tends to be poor at capturing zooplankton or larger food particles. Supplemental feeding is generally considered optional rather than required in well-lit systems.

goodWith: most soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: sensitive SPS corals placed in close proximity

summary: Nephthea (now Litophyton sp.) is a photosynthetic soft tree coral from the Indo-Pacific, generally considered more beginner-friendly than most corals once acclimated. Moderate light and flow; fast-growing branching colonies.
careNotes:
TAXONOMY NOTE — 2012 reclassification: The aquarium trade still widely uses the genus name Nephthea, but a molecular and morphological revision (Ofwegen & Groenenberg, 2007; formally adopted ~2012) synonymized Nephthea with the earlier-named genus Litophyton Forskål, 1775. Current accepted nomenclature for these corals is Litophyton sp. The scientificName field uses Litophyton sp. to reflect this; the trade name Nephthea is preserved in alsoKnownAs. Many retailer listings continue to use Nephthea or the abbreviated 'Nepthea'.

CRITICAL DISTINCTION FROM DENDRONEPHTHYA: Nephthea/Litophyton corals are zooxanthellate (photosynthetic) — they host symbiotic zooxanthellae algae and derive the majority of their energy from light. This is the fundamental difference from the superficially similar Dendronephthya (Carnation Coral / Cauliflower Coral), which is azooxanthellate (non-photosynthetic) and requires heavy particulate feeding to survive. In the aquarium trade, Dendronephthya is notoriously difficult to keep alive; Nephthea/Litophyton is significantly more forgiving. Do not confuse the two based on appearance alone.

Naming confusion: Some vendors label 'Cauliflower Coral' for Dendronephthya and some for Nephthea. Nephthea is also sometimes confused with Kenya Tree Coral (Capnella sp.), which belongs to a different genus in the same family.

ACCLIMATION: Sources note that Nephthea/Litophyton tends to be sensitive during shipping, handling, and initial acclimation, despite being hardy once established. New specimens should be introduced carefully and given time to settle before drawing conclusions about their health.

SHEDDING BEHAVIOR: These corals periodically shed a waxy or mucus-like outer coating as a natural cleaning process. Adequate water flow assists shedding. A coral that appears deflated or 'gooey' for 1–3 days may simply be shedding — this is normal behavior and is not necessarily a sign of illness.

CHEMICAL WARFARE: Nephthea/Litophyton releases allelopathic compounds (terpenes) that can irritate neighboring corals, particularly sensitive SPS. Running activated carbon and maintaining physical spacing (at least 10–15 cm from adjacent colonies) is generally advised.

LIGHTING: Tends to extend polyps better under moderate rather than intense light. Sources report optimal extension at 75–200 PAR. Specimens placed in higher-intensity zones should be acclimated gradually over several weeks. Under extreme illumination, the colony may darken as zooxanthellae density increases.

FLOW: Best in moderate, non-turbulent flow — brisk enough to keep the branches gently swaying but not crashing wave action. Sources note that Nephthea dislikes strong wave-action and does best in moderate laminar or gyre-type currents.

GROWTH: Widely reported as a fast-growing soft coral. Colonies sold as small frags (2–5 cm) can develop into sizable branching colonies within months under good conditions. Growth rate makes occasional pruning likely in smaller tanks.

WATER PARAMETERS: Generally considered tolerant of a wider parameter range than SPS corals, though stable NSW-type parameters are still advisable. Temperature disagreement noted across sources: BRS (ECC listing) states 75–78°F (23.9–25.6°C); BRS (ORA listing) states 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C); Whitlyn Aquatics states 74–80°F (23.3–26.7°C); Reeflex states 24–28°C. Averaged range of 23–27°C adopted here.

ALKALINITY: BRS ECC listing: 8–9.5 dKH; BRS ORA listing: 8–12 dKH. The broader ORA range likely reflects general reef parameters rather than Nephthea-specific needs. Conservative midpoint of 8–10 dKH adopted.
breedingNotes: Fragging is commonly practiced and straightforward — branches can be cut with scissors or bone cutters and attached to frag plugs or rubble. The fast growth rate makes Nephthea/Litophyton one of the more commonly traded soft coral frags in the hobby. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning in the wild but is not deliberately practiced in captivity.

primary.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ECC Koji Pink Nephthea
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters, lighting PAR range, flow, placement, and care level. Scientific name listed as Nephthea chabrolii on product page; updated to Litophyton sp. per current taxonomy.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ORA Nepthea Coral
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters. Temperature 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C), salinity 1.025–1.026, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm, lighting 150–250 PAR. Slight disagreement on temperature upper bound and kH range vs. ECC listing; averaged for final values.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – Keeping Nephthea Corals
additional.notes: Confirmed zooxanthellate (photosynthetic) status, Indo-Pacific native range, phytoplankton feeding notes, acclimation sensitivity, and shedding behavior. Care level rated beginner-friendly once established. Noted photosynthetic distinction from Dendronephthya.

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Neon Green Nephthea
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (Fiji, Tonga, Solomon Islands, Great Barrier Reef), flow requirements, feeding mode. Lighting reported as 50–150 PAR (lower than BRS); BRS PAR range adopted as broader consensus. Confirmed allelopathic terpene release.

additional.name: Reeflex – Litophyton arboreum
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy confirmation (Litophyton arboreum Forskål, 1775), native range expansion (Red Sea, Egypt, Israel, Saudi Arabia, Papua New Guinea, New Caledonia), depth range (1–24 m), temperature (24–28°C), and minimum tank size indication (~300 L). Colony size up to 80 cm from this source.
